skill.name: agent-tools
skill.description: Use the inference.sh CLI to discover and run hosted AI apps for image, video, LLM, search, 3D, audio, and Twitter/X workflows.
skill.summary: Run and manage inference.sh AI apps from an AI coding assistant.
skill.author: inference-sh-6

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command and network patterns, but most are markdown examples for the documented infsh CLI rather than executable code. Confirmed concerns are the remote installer piped to sh, authenticated cloud execution, API key configuration, and minor filesystem writes for shell completion; no prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to download an installer from a remote URL and pipe it directly to sh. This is a supply-chain risk because a changed endpoint, compromised transport path, or compromised host could execute arbitrary installer code before local review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is directly present in installation examples. The surrounding documentation offers a manual install path and checksum discussion, so this is a real but not clearly malicious risk.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Cloud App Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to run hosted inference.sh apps over the network using the local infsh CLI. This is expected behavior, but prompts, files, generated outputs, and Twitter/X actions may leave the local environment depending on the selected app.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill purpose and examples clearly involve network execution of remote apps. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but users need to understand data disclosure and side-effect risks.

medium_findings.title: API Key Configuration Guidance
medium_findings.description: The documentation tells users to configure INFSH_API_KEY for CI/CD and notes that it overrides local config. This is normal authentication guidance, but exposed environment variables could allow unauthorized use of the account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly document an API key environment variable. There is no evidence the skill reads or exfiltrates the key itself, so the risk is credential handling rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Trigger Static Detections
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are CLI examples inside documentation. They are not executable skill code, and the declared tool permission is limited to Bash(infsh *), but the examples still guide users to run commands with cloud side effects.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are fenced markdown command examples and command tables. Context strongly supports false positives for direct code execution inside the skill.

low_findings.title: Checksum References Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography, but the reviewed content references SHA-256 checksum verification for downloaded binaries. This is a safety control, not a weak cryptographic algorithm use in skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The clearest reviewed occurrence is SHA-256 checksum documentation. Some analyzer line mappings point at tables, so there is no evidence of implemented weak cryptography.

low_findings.title: Shell Completion File Writes
low_findings.description: The CLI reference includes shell completion examples that write generated completion files under system or user shell configuration paths. This is common CLI setup behavior, but users should review paths before running commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem writes are visible in documentation examples and are limited to installation or shell completion paths. There is no evidence of hidden persistence or arbitrary file modification.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Remote Script to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: Installation examples use curl with a shell pipe. This pattern should be replaced with manual download, checksum verification, and explicit execution when possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static pattern is present and semantically meaningful. It is an installer convenience pattern, not proof of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Service Actions From CLI Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include remote model execution, web search, and Twitter/X automation through inference.sh apps. These operations can disclose prompts or create external side effects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The capability is explicitly documented and expected. The risk depends on user-selected apps and inputs rather than hidden skill behavior.

content.user_title: Run AI Apps with inference.sh CLI
content.value_statement: Teams often need one interface for many hosted AI models and media tools. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through inference.sh CLI commands for discovery, authentication, execution, and task tracking.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and authenticate the inference.sh CLI.
Lists, searches, filters, and inspects available inference.sh apps.
Runs hosted apps with inline JSON or saved input files.
Tracks long-running tasks and retrieves results from task IDs.
Provides examples for image, video, LLM, search, 3D, audio, and Twitter/X apps.
Documents API key setup, shell completions, and related specialized skills.
limitations: Requires the infsh CLI, an inference.sh account, and valid authentication.
Remote apps may send prompts, files, or outputs to hosted services.
Costs, quotas, latency, and model availability depend on the inference.sh account and platform.
The skill documents workflows; it does not implement local model inference itself.

use_cases.title: Prototype multimodal AI workflows
use_cases.description: Quickly test image, video, audio, search, and LLM apps from one CLI workflow.

use_cases.title: Operate hosted AI tasks
use_cases.description: Run apps with saved inputs, submit long jobs without waiting, and retrieve task results later.

use_cases.title: Explore model options
use_cases.description: Search available apps by category, inspect schemas, and compare model-specific capabilities before integration.

prompt_templates.title: Find an app
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agent-tools skill to find inference.sh apps for image generation. Show the command I should run and explain the result.

prompt_templates.title: Run a sample task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agent-tools skill to generate a sample input for a video app, then show how to run it with infsh.

prompt_templates.title: Track a long task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agent-tools skill to run a long video generation task without waiting, then show how to check the task status later.

prompt_templates.title: Compare tool choices
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agent-tools skill to compare inference.sh app options for search plus LLM summarization. Include data handling and authentication considerations.

output_examples.input: Find a fast image generation app and show the next command.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests searching image apps, inspecting a selected app, generating sample input, and running it with the infsh app run command.

output_examples.input: Run a video job in the background and check it later.
output_examples.output: The assistant provides a no-wait run command, explains that a task ID will be returned, and gives the follow-up task status command.
The assistant explains API key usage, warns to store the key securely, and points to the CLI authentication documentation.

best_practices: Review app schemas before sending prompts, files, or private data to hosted apps.
Use saved input files for repeatable runs and version-controlled workflow changes.
Prefer manual installer verification when installing the CLI in sensitive environments.
anti_patterns: Do not paste secrets into inline JSON prompts or command history.
Do not run Twitter/X automation commands without reviewing the exact text and account context.
Do not pipe remote installers to a shell on production systems without independent verification.

faq.question: What does this skill help an assistant do?
faq.answer: It helps an assistant use the inference.sh CLI to discover apps, run tasks, and retrieve results.

faq.question: Does it include the inference.sh CLI binary?
faq.answer: No. It provides installation and usage guidance for the separate infsh CLI.

faq.question: Do I need an account?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflows require inference.sh authentication or an API key.

faq.question: Can it run local models?
faq.answer: No. The workflows call hosted inference.sh apps through the CLI.

faq.question: What data may leave my machine?
faq.answer: Prompts, input files, generated outputs, and app parameters may be sent to hosted services when you run apps.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate CLI documentation, but it includes remote installation, API keys, network execution, and external side effects.
